questions.stem: A 68-year-old woman receives a retrobulbar block with lidocaine and bupivacaine for cataract surgery. Approximately 8 minutes after the injection she becomes confused and then unresponsive, develops slurred speech followed by apnea, and her heart rate falls to 38 beats per minute. The contralateral (non-blocked) eye is noted to have lost vision. What is the most likely cause of this presentation?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Inadvertent spread of local anesthetic into the subarachnoid space producing brainstem anesthesia
choices.rationale: Correct. The constellation of delayed-onset (roughly 10 minutes) loss of consciousness, contralateral amaurosis, apnea, and bradycardia is classic brainstem anesthesia. StatPearls describes this as local anesthetic tracking along the optic nerve sheath into the subarachnoid space (or retrograde vascular spread) reaching the brainstem, requiring airway support and supportive care until the block resolves.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Retrobulbar hemorrhage causing acute orbital compartment syndrome
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Retrobulbar hemorrhage produces a tense, proptotic, ecchymotic orbit with rising intraocular pressure, not contralateral vision loss, apnea, and loss of consciousness. It is a local orbital emergency, not a central neurologic event.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Systemic local anesthetic toxicity from intravascular bupivacaine causing cardiac arrest
choices.rationale: Incorrect. While bupivacaine cardiotoxicity is a concern, the small volume used in a retrobulbar block rarely produces systemic LAST, and LAST would not explain the hallmark contralateral amaurosis. The contralateral vision loss points specifically to CSF spread to the optic chiasm/brainstem rather than systemic toxicity.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A vasovagal episode triggered by the pain of the injection
choices.rationale: Incorrect. A vasovagal reaction causes transient bradycardia and hypotension that resolves quickly with recumbency; it does not cause sustained apnea, unconsciousness, or loss of vision in the opposite eye.

choices.label: E
choices.text: An acute embolic stroke unrelated to the block
choices.rationale: Incorrect. The tight temporal link to the injection (symptoms minutes later), bilateral visual involvement, apnea, and full recovery expected over a few hours fit brainstem anesthesia from anesthetic spread far better than a spontaneous embolic stroke.

questions.explanation: Brainstem anesthesia is a recognized, potentially lethal complication of retrobulbar block, occurring in roughly 0.3-0.8% of cases. Local anesthetic is thought to enter the subarachnoid space either by direct penetration of the optic nerve sheath (which is contiguous with the dura and CSF) or by retrograde intra-arterial spread, reaching the midbrain and brainstem. The classic picture evolves about 10-15 minutes after injection and includes contralateral amaurosis (anesthetic crossing at the chiasm), confusion, loss of consciousness, apnea, and cardiovascular instability including bradycardia. Management is supportive: secure the airway, ventilate, and provide hemodynamic support in a monitored setting until the effect resolves over a few hours. Anticipating and recognizing this complication, and never leaving a freshly blocked patient unmonitored, are the key teaching points.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man sustains a penetrating open-globe injury at work 90 minutes after eating a full meal and is scheduled for urgent surgical repair. He has no other injuries and a reassuring airway exam. Which anesthetic plan best balances aspiration risk against the need to avoid further increases in intraocular pressure?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Rapid sequence induction with adequate-dose propofol and rocuronium, with a deliberately deep plane before laryngoscopy
choices.rationale: Correct. A full stomach mandates rapid sequence induction to protect against aspiration, while open-globe repair requires avoiding spikes in intraocular pressure. Rocuronium provides rapid intubating conditions without the IOP rise associated with succinylcholine, and ensuring a deep plane of anesthesia before laryngoscopy blunts the IOP increase from intubation. This addresses both competing priorities.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Inhalational induction with sevoflurane and spontaneous ventilation to avoid muscle relaxants entirely
choices.rationale: Incorrect. A slow inhalational induction provides no aspiration protection in a full-stomach patient and risks coughing, bucking, and breath-holding that sharply raise intraocular pressure. It fails the primary goal of securing the airway rapidly and safely.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Awake fiberoptic intubation with the patient lightly sedated and topicalized
choices.rationale: Incorrect. With a reassuring airway, awake fiberoptic intubation is unnecessary and the gagging, coughing, and straining it provokes would dangerously elevate intraocular pressure and risk extrusion of intraocular contents. It is reserved for anticipated difficult airways, not routine open-globe cases.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Rapid sequence induction using succinylcholine because its rapid onset and offset make it the safest relaxant for the eye
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Although succinylcholine offers rapid onset, it transiently raises intraocular pressure (roughly 10 mmHg), which is the specific effect to avoid in an open globe. With rocuronium available as a fast-onset alternative that does not raise IOP, succinylcholine is not the preferred choice here.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Proceed under retrobulbar block alone to avoid the airway risks of general anesthesia
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Injecting local anesthetic into the orbit of an open globe raises intraorbital pressure and risks expelling intraocular contents, so regional block is contraindicated for open-globe repair. The standard approach is controlled general anesthesia with a secured airway.

questions.explanation: The open globe with a full stomach is the classic competing-priorities scenario: the full stomach demands rapid sequence induction to prevent aspiration, while the open globe demands avoidance of any rise in intraocular pressure that could extrude intraocular contents and worsen visual outcome. Succinylcholine, the traditional RSI relaxant, transiently increases IOP and is therefore relatively avoided when an equally rapid non-depolarizing alternative exists. Rocuronium at intubating doses provides fast onset without raising IOP and is recommended in this setting. A deep plane of anesthesia and adequate dosing before laryngoscopy blunt the additional IOP spike caused by intubation. Regional orbital blocks are contraindicated in open-globe injuries because they raise intraorbital pressure. The teaching point is to deliver a controlled RSI with a relaxant and depth that protect both the lungs and the eye.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man requires general anesthesia for an inguinal hernia repair. He underwent pars plana vitrectomy with an intravitreal sulfur hexafluoride (SF6) gas tamponade for retinal detachment 9 days ago. Which intraoperative anesthetic decision is most important to protect his vision?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Avoid nitrous oxide for the entire case
choices.rationale: Correct. Nitrous oxide is far more soluble than the gases in an intraocular bubble, so it diffuses into the closed SF6 bubble faster than the SF6 leaves, expanding the bubble and sharply raising intraocular pressure. This can exceed central retinal artery perfusion pressure and cause irreversible ischemic vision loss, so nitrous oxide must be avoided while intraocular gas remains.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Avoid succinylcholine and use rocuronium instead
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Succinylcholine causes only a transient, modest IOP rise and is not the principal hazard here. The catastrophic and specific danger in a patient with an intraocular gas bubble is nitrous oxide-induced bubble expansion, which dwarfs any concern about the choice of muscle relaxant.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Avoid ketamine because of its sympathomimetic effect on intraocular pressure
choices.rationale: Incorrect. The historical concern that ketamine raises IOP has largely been refuted, and in any case it is not the gas-related hazard. The intravitreal gas bubble makes nitrous oxide the agent that must be avoided.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Avoid all volatile anesthetics because they expand the intraocular gas bubble
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Volatile agents such as sevoflurane and isoflurane actually tend to lower intraocular pressure and do not diffuse into and expand the gas bubble the way nitrous oxide does. Eliminating volatiles is unnecessary; eliminating nitrous oxide is what matters.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Avoid positive pressure ventilation and keep the patient breathing spontaneously
choices.rationale: Incorrect. The mode of ventilation does not drive bubble expansion. The relevant factor is the inspired gas mixture: nitrous oxide diffusing into the SF6 bubble is the threat, not whether ventilation is spontaneous or controlled.

questions.explanation: After intravitreal injection of an expansile gas such as sulfur hexafluoride (SF6) or perfluoropropane (C3F8), the eye contains a closed gas bubble. Nitrous oxide is roughly 34 times more soluble than nitrogen and diffuses into this bubble much faster than the heavier gas can diffuse out. The result is rapid bubble expansion and a steep rise in intraocular pressure that can exceed central retinal artery perfusion pressure, producing retinal ischemia and permanent blindness. Nitrous oxide is therefore contraindicated while intraocular gas remains, which can be days to weeks for SF6 and longer for C3F8. Patients are often advised to carry a warning card. The teaching point is that any patient with a recent intraocular gas injection must not receive nitrous oxide; the safe choice is to omit it entirely for the case.

questions.stem: A healthy 30-year-old man is scheduled for an elective inguinal hernia repair under general anesthesia at 1:00 PM. He asks the preoperative nurse what he may eat or drink and when he must stop. According to the American Society of Anesthesiologists preoperative fasting recommendations for healthy patients undergoing elective procedures, which instruction is correct?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Clear liquids are permitted up to 2 hours before the procedure
choices.rationale: Correct. The ASA recommends a minimum fast of 2 hours for clear liquids (water, pulp-free juice, clear tea, black coffee, carbonated beverages) in healthy patients before elective procedures requiring sedation or general anesthesia.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Clear liquids must be stopped at least 6 hours before the procedure
choices.rationale: Six hours is the recommended minimum fast for a light meal (and for infant formula/nonhuman milk), not for clear liquids. Applying the light-meal interval to clear liquids prolongs fasting unnecessarily and increases patient discomfort without added safety benefit.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A light meal such as toast is permitted up to 2 hours before the procedure
choices.rationale: A light meal requires a minimum fast of 6 hours, not 2 hours. The 2-hour interval applies only to clear liquids. Eating solids 2 hours before induction leaves substantial gastric residual and raises aspiration risk.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Fried or fatty foods are permitted up to 6 hours before the procedure
choices.rationale: Fried foods, fatty foods, and meat delay gastric emptying and require a minimum fast of 8 hours or more, not 6 hours. The 6-hour interval is reserved for a light meal.

choices.label: E
choices.text: He must remain strictly NPO (nothing by mouth) from midnight the night before
choices.rationale: The traditional 'NPO after midnight' rule is outdated for elective cases in healthy patients. Modern ASA guidance allows clear liquids until 2 hours preprocedure; blanket midnight fasting causes prolonged dehydration and discomfort without reducing aspiration risk.

questions.explanation: ASA preoperative fasting guidance aims to minimize gastric volume and thus the risk of perioperative pulmonary aspiration while avoiding the harms of excessive fasting. The standard minimum intervals for healthy patients before elective procedures are: 2 hours for clear liquids, 4 hours for human breast milk, 6 hours for nonhuman milk/infant formula/a light meal, and 8 hours or more for fried or fatty foods and meat. Presence of food or liquid in the stomach at induction is one of the greatest risk factors for aspiration, which carries markedly increased mortality and ICU admission. The once-universal 'NPO after midnight' practice has been replaced by these graded intervals; a patient with a 1:00 PM case can safely drink clear liquids until 11:00 AM.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man is evaluated before an elective open infrarenal abdominal aortic aneurysm repair (a suprainguinal vascular, intraperitoneal procedure). His history includes a prior ischemic stroke with full recovery, hypertension controlled on two agents, and type 2 diabetes managed with oral metformin. Serum creatinine is 1.4 mg/dL. He has no history of myocardial infarction, heart failure, or angina. Using the Revised Cardiac Risk Index (RCRI/Lee index), how many risk factors does this patient have?

choices.label: A
choices.rationale: This undercounts. The patient has at least the high-risk surgery factor plus cerebrovascular disease, so a count of 1 misses one qualifying predictor.

choices.label: B
choices.rationale: Correct. He meets exactly two RCRI predictors: high-risk surgery (suprainguinal vascular/intraperitoneal) and a history of cerebrovascular disease (prior stroke). His diabetes is treated with an oral agent, not insulin, so it does not count; his creatinine of 1.4 mg/dL is below the 2 mg/dL threshold; and he has no ischemic heart disease or heart failure.

choices.label: C
choices.rationale: This overcounts, most likely by erroneously counting the oral-agent diabetes or the creatinine of 1.4 mg/dL. RCRI counts only insulin-dependent diabetes and only creatinine greater than 2 mg/dL, so neither qualifies here.

choices.label: D
choices.rationale: This overcounts substantially, likely by also counting hypertension. Hypertension is not one of the six RCRI predictors, and the diabetes and creatinine do not meet RCRI criteria either.

choices.label: E
choices.rationale: This counts nearly all candidate conditions as positive. Only high-risk surgery and cerebrovascular disease qualify; ischemic heart disease and heart failure are absent, hypertension is not an RCRI factor, and the diabetes and creatinine do not meet thresholds.

questions.explanation: The Revised Cardiac Risk Index (Lee index) uses six equally weighted predictors of major perioperative cardiac events: (1) high-risk surgery (suprainguinal vascular, intraperitoneal, or intrathoracic), (2) history of ischemic/coronary heart disease, (3) history of congestive heart failure, (4) history of cerebrovascular disease, (5) insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus, and (6) renal insufficiency with serum creatinine greater than 2 mg/dL. Two common distractor traps are counting diabetes treated with oral agents (only insulin-treated diabetes qualifies) and counting mildly elevated creatinine (the threshold is strictly greater than 2 mg/dL). Hypertension, although clinically important, is not an RCRI predictor. This patient scores 2 (high-risk surgery plus cerebrovascular disease), placing him in an elevated-risk category that warrants careful perioperative cardiac assessment.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old man arrives unconscious after a high-speed motor vehicle collision with a tense, distended abdomen and progressive hypotension despite resuscitation. He requires emergent exploratory laparotomy under general anesthesia for presumed intra-abdominal hemorrhage. He has no identification, no advance directive, and no family or surrogate can be located despite reasonable efforts. Which principle most appropriately governs proceeding with anesthesia and surgery?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Consent is implied under the emergency exception, and treatment may proceed
choices.rationale: Correct. The emergency exception (implied consent) applies when an immediate threat to life or health exists, the intervention is necessary to address it, and express consent cannot be obtained from the patient or an authorized surrogate. All three conditions are met here, so the team may proceed.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Surgery must be delayed until a court order or ethics committee approval is obtained
choices.rationale: Requiring a court order or committee approval before life-saving emergency surgery would cause fatal delay. The emergency exception exists precisely so that immediately necessary treatment is not withheld while pursuing formal authorization.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The anesthesiologist may proceed only after two physicians document that the patient lacks capacity
choices.rationale: While documenting incapacity is good practice, the emergency exception does not hinge on a formal two-physician capacity attestation. The patient is unconscious and the threat is immediate; insisting on this procedural step before acting is not the governing principle and could dangerously delay care.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Because the patient cannot consent, only comfort measures may be provided until a surrogate is found
choices.rationale: Limiting an otherwise salvageable trauma patient to comfort measures misapplies the inability to consent. The emergency exception authorizes necessary life-saving intervention; withholding it would cause preventable death.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Written informed consent is legally required for all general anesthesia, so the case cannot proceed
choices.rationale: Although informed consent is normally required, it is not absolute. In a life-threatening emergency where the patient lacks capacity and no surrogate is available, consent is presumed/implied, and the absence of a signed form does not bar necessary treatment.

questions.explanation: Valid informed consent requires disclosure (nature of the condition and proposed treatment, risks, benefits, and alternatives), patient capacity (ability to understand and retain information, weigh options, and communicate a choice), and voluntariness. When a patient lacks capacity and faces an immediate threat to life or health, and no authorized surrogate can be reached despite reasonable efforts, the emergency (implied consent) exception permits clinicians to provide necessary treatment, because a reasonable person would be presumed to consent. This exception is narrow: it applies only when obtaining express consent is genuinely impossible due to the emergency and incapacity. It does not require a court order, an ethics committee, or a formal two-physician attestation before life-saving care proceeds.

Distractor rationales are accurate: B (court order/ethics committee) would cause fatal delay and is not required in a true emergency; C (two-physician capacity documentation) is not the governing principle for an obviously unconscious patient; D (comfort measures only) would constitute abandonment and contradicts the exception authorizing full life-saving care; E (written consent legally required for all general anesthesia) directly contradicts the source. Medicine and law are sound for an unconscious trauma patient with life-threatening hemorrhage and no reachable surrogate.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old man with severe anemia (hemoglobin 6.0 g/dL) is undergoing urgent surgery. His SaO2 is 99% on the pulse oximeter and an arterial blood gas shows PaO2 of 240 mmHg on supplemental oxygen. His cardiac output is normal at 5 L/min. The surgeon asks why the patient still appears to have marginal oxygen delivery despite the very high PaO2. Which statement best explains the patient's oxygen delivery status?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The high PaO2 contributes little to total oxygen content because dissolved oxygen accounts for only about 2% of blood oxygen, and the low hemoglobin sharply reduces oxygen-carrying capacity
choices.rationale: Correct. Arterial oxygen content is CaO2 = (1.34 x Hgb x SaO2) + (0.003 x PaO2). The dissolved term (0.003 x 240 = ~0.72 mL/dL) is trivial; ~98% of oxygen is hemoglobin-bound. With Hgb only 6 g/dL, carrying capacity is roughly halved, so CaO2 and therefore DO2 (CaO2 x cardiac output) are low despite the high PaO2.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A PaO2 of 240 mmHg fully saturates hemoglobin and dissolves enough additional oxygen to compensate for the low hemoglobin
choices.rationale: Incorrect. This is the central misconception. Once SaO2 is ~99-100%, raising PaO2 adds only dissolved oxygen (0.003 x PaO2 per dL), which is negligible (<1 mL/dL) and cannot offset the large deficit in hemoglobin-bound oxygen.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Oxygen delivery is determined primarily by PaO2 rather than by hemoglobin concentration
choices.rationale: Incorrect. PaO2 drives hemoglobin saturation but, once saturation is near-maximal, hemoglobin concentration is the dominant determinant of oxygen content. Delivery (DO2 = CaO2 x cardiac output) falls with anemia even when PaO2 is high.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Because cardiac output is normal, oxygen delivery must be adequate regardless of hemoglobin
choices.rationale: Incorrect. DO2 is the product of cardiac output AND arterial oxygen content. A normal cardiac output cannot guarantee adequate delivery when oxygen content is reduced by severe anemia; compensatory increases in output would be required.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The pulse oximeter reading of 99% confirms that oxygen content is normal
choices.rationale: Incorrect. SaO2 reflects the percentage of available hemoglobin that is saturated, not the absolute amount of oxygen carried. With low hemoglobin, oxygen content is low even at 99% saturation.

questions.explanation: Oxygen delivery (DO2) equals cardiac output multiplied by arterial oxygen content (CaO2). CaO2 = 1.34 x [Hgb] x (SaO2/100) + 0.003 x PaO2. About 98% of blood oxygen is bound to hemoglobin and only ~2% is dissolved in plasma, so PaO2 contributes meaningfully to content only through its effect on saturation. Once hemoglobin is fully saturated, further increases in PaO2 add only trivial dissolved oxygen. In severe anemia, the dominant problem is reduced carrying capacity; the fix is increasing hemoglobin (transfusion) and/or cardiac output, not PaO2.

source.title: Physiology, Oxygen Transport - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf

verdict.reason:
VERIFIED — publish OK.

SOURCE: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK538336/ (Physiology, Oxygen Transport — StatPearls/NCBI Bookshelf) loads, is publicly accessible, and directly supports the question. It states ~98% of blood oxygen is hemoglobin-bound and ~2% is dissolved in plasma; gives the oxygen-content equation CaO2 = 1.34·[Hgb]·(SaO2/100) + 0.003·PaO2; gives oxygen delivery DO2 = CO · CaO2; and states anemia reduces oxygen-carrying capacity.

For this patient (Hb 6.0, SaO2 99%, PaO2 240): CaO2 ≈ 1.34×6×0.99 + 0.003×240 = 7.96 + 0.72 ≈ 8.7 mL/dL vs normal ~20 mL/dL; DO2 ≈ 8.7 × 50 dL/min ≈ ~430 mL/min vs normal ~1000 mL/min — i.e., marginal delivery despite high PaO2 because the dissolved component is tiny and low Hb collapses carrying capacity. DISTRACTORS all accurately wrong: B — dissolved O2 (0.72 mL/dL) cannot offset the ~12 mL/dL deficit from anemia. C — delivery depends primarily on Hb-bound O2, not PaO2. D — normal cardiac output cannot compensate for severely reduced CaO2. E — pulse oximetry reflects saturation %, not oxygen content; 99% saturation of low Hb still yields low content.

questions.stem: During a discussion of carbon dioxide carriage, a student asks how most CO2 produced by metabolizing tissues is transported from the periphery to the lungs. In peripheral capillaries, CO2 diffuses into red blood cells where an enzyme accelerates its hydration, and the resulting anion leaves the cell in exchange for another ion. Which statement correctly describes the predominant mechanism of CO2 transport in blood?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Most CO2 is converted to bicarbonate by carbonic anhydrase in red blood cells, and bicarbonate exits in exchange for chloride (the chloride shift)
choices.rationale: Correct. Roughly 80-90% of CO2 is carried as bicarbonate. Carbonic anhydrase in red cells catalyzes CO2 + H2O to H2CO3, which dissociates to H+ and HCO3-. Bicarbonate then exits the cell via an HCO3-/Cl- exchanger (the chloride shift, or Hamburger phenomenon), raising venous bicarbonate and lowering venous chloride.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Most CO2 is carried dissolved in plasma as molecular CO2
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Only about 10% of CO2 is carried in the dissolved form. Dissolved CO2 sets the PaCO2 and is the substrate for bicarbonate formation, but it is not the predominant transport form.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Most CO2 is carried bound to the heme iron of hemoglobin, competing directly with oxygen
choices.rationale: Incorrect. CO2 binds the terminal amino groups of the globin chains (forming carbaminohemoglobin), not the heme iron, and this accounts for only about 5-10% of transport. Oxygen binds heme iron; CO2 does not compete there.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Most CO2 is carried as carbonic acid (H2CO3) circulating in plasma
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Carbonic acid is only a transient intermediate; it rapidly dissociates to H+ and bicarbonate and is present in negligible concentration. It is not a major transport pool.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Most CO2 is carried as carbaminohemoglobin, which increases when hemoglobin is oxygenated
choices.rationale: Incorrect on two counts: carbaminohemoglobin is a minor pool (~5-10%), and by the Haldane effect deoxygenated (not oxygenated) hemoglobin carries more CO2. Oxygenation reduces CO2 affinity, facilitating CO2 unloading in the lungs.

questions.explanation: CO2 is transported in three forms: as bicarbonate (~80-90%, the dominant route), as carbaminohemoglobin bound to globin amino termini (~5-10%), and dissolved in plasma (~10%). In peripheral tissues, carbonic anhydrase inside red cells rapidly hydrates CO2 to carbonic acid, which dissociates into H+ (buffered by hemoglobin) and bicarbonate; bicarbonate exits in exchange for chloride (the chloride shift). The Haldane effect means deoxygenated hemoglobin carries more CO2, enhancing peripheral uptake and pulmonary release.

verdict.reason: The cited source (StatPearls NBK532988, publicly accessible, loaded successfully) fully supports correct answer A. It states ~80% of CO2 is transported as bicarbonate, that carbonic anhydrase in RBCs catalyzes CO2 hydration to carbonic acid which dissociates to bicarbonate, and that bicarbonate exits the RBC via a HCO3-/Cl- transporter in exchange for chloride (the chloride shift). The stem is medically accurate and unambiguous (CO2 diffuses into RBCs, enzyme accelerates hydration, resulting anion leaves in exchange for another ion). All distractors are correctly wrong: B (dissolved, only ~10%), C (CO2 binds the amino terminus of globin, not heme iron, and does not directly compete with O2), D (H2CO3 is a transient intermediate, not the transport form), and E (per the Haldane effect, carbaminohemoglobin formation DECREASES with oxygenation, not increases). The question is sound and safe to publish.

questions.stem: A 64-year-old man under general anesthesia has his mean arterial pressure (MAP) rise transiently from 95 to 130 mmHg during a period of intense surgical stimulation. His renal blood flow and glomerular filtration rate remain nearly constant. Which mechanism is primarily responsible for the rapid, early component of this autoregulatory response?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Myogenic constriction of the afferent arteriole in response to increased wall stretch
choices.rationale: Correct. The myogenic mechanism is the rapid component: increased transmural pressure stretches the afferent arteriolar smooth muscle, which responds by contracting. This increases preglomerular resistance and keeps glomerular capillary pressure, renal blood flow, and GFR stable as MAP rises within the autoregulatory range (~80-180 mmHg).

choices.label: B
choices.text: Tubuloglomerular feedback driven by increased NaCl sensed at the macula densa
choices.rationale: Incorrect as the EARLY component. Tubuloglomerular feedback also contributes to autoregulation, but it is the slower mechanism: it requires increased tubular flow and NaCl delivery to be sensed by the macula densa, which then signals (via ATP/adenosine) afferent constriction. The rapid initial response is myogenic.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Dilation of the efferent arteriole mediated by the macula densa
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Autoregulation acts mainly on the preglomerular (afferent) resistance. Efferent dilation would lower glomerular pressure but is not the principal autoregulatory mechanism for a rise in MAP, and the macula densa primarily modulates the afferent arteriole.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A surge in renin and angiotensin II constricting the afferent arteriole
choices.rationale: Incorrect. A pressure rise tends to reduce renin release, and angiotensin II preferentially constricts the EFFERENT arteriole. The renin-angiotensin system supports GFR during hypotension, not the rapid response to a pressure increase.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Sympathetic-mediated vasoconstriction of the renal artery
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Renal autoregulation is intrinsic and persists even in a denervated or isolated kidney; it does not require sympathetic input. Sympathetic tone modulates renal blood flow but is not the mechanism keeping GFR constant across the autoregulatory pressure range.

questions.explanation: Renal autoregulation keeps renal blood flow and GFR nearly constant across a MAP range of roughly 80-180 mmHg via two mechanisms acting on the afferent arteriole. The myogenic response is rapid: increased transmural pressure stretches afferent smooth muscle, triggering contraction within seconds. Tubuloglomerular feedback is slower: the macula densa senses increased distal NaCl delivery and signals afferent constriction via ATP/adenosine. Both raise preglomerular resistance to buffer glomerular pressure; the immediate component is myogenic.

source.title: Physiology, Renal Blood Flow and Filtration - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf

verdict.reason:
The cited URL (https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK482248/, StatPearls "Physiology, Renal Blood Flow and Filtration") loads, is publicly accessible, and is on-topic. It directly supports the keyed answer A: it states "During the increased stretch, the renal afferent arterioles contract to decrease GFR" (the myogenic mechanism) and confirms the autoregulatory range (~80-180 mmHg), so RBF/GFR holding constant as MAP rises 95→130 is consistent.

The medicine is correct and unambiguous. The myogenic response is the rapid/early component of renal autoregulation (afferent arteriolar smooth muscle constricts within ~1-2 s in response to increased wall stretch from elevated pressure), which is exactly what option A states. Tubuloglomerular feedback (option B) is genuine autoregulation but is the slower component (acts over ~10+ s via macula densa NaCl sensing), so it is correctly used as a distractor for "rapid, early." The source notes the page describes both mechanisms operating together but does not itself use the explicit "rapid vs slow" wording; however, the myogenic-fast / TGF-slow distinction is universally accepted core physiology, so the keyed answer remains correct and unambiguous.

Distractor rationales are accurate: C (macula densa causing efferent dilation to maintain flow) is fabricated/incorrect; D is physiologically backwards (rising pressure suppresses renin, and angiotensin II preferentially constricts the EFFERENT arteriole); E (sympathetic vasoconstriction) is extrinsic regulation, not the intrinsic autoregulatory response and not the rapid autoregulatory mechanism. The clinical anesthesia framing does not introduce ambiguity. All four gating criteria are met.

questions.stem: A 67-year-old woman is undergoing a right thoracotomy requiring one-lung ventilation, with the right (nondependent) lung collapsed. After the operative lung is allowed to collapse, her arterial oxygenation is better than expected for the degree of shunt. Which physiologic mechanism best explains the limitation of hypoxemia in this setting, and what is the direct vascular event involved?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction diverts blood from the poorly ventilated lung toward better-ventilated regions, mediated by inhibition of oxygen-sensitive potassium channels and calcium influx in pulmonary artery smooth muscle
choices.rationale: Correct. Alveolar hypoxia in the collapsed lung triggers hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction (HPV), which redirects blood flow away from poorly ventilated lung toward well-ventilated lung, improving ventilation-perfusion matching and limiting shunt/hypoxemia. The mechanism involves inhibition of voltage-gated (oxygen-sensitive) potassium channels, smooth muscle depolarization, and voltage-gated calcium channel-mediated calcium entry causing vasoconstriction.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Hypoxic pulmonary vasodilation in the collapsed lung increases its perfusion to capture residual oxygen
choices.rationale: Incorrect. The pulmonary response to alveolar hypoxia is vasoCONSTRICTION, not vasodilation. Increasing perfusion to a non-ventilated lung would worsen shunt and hypoxemia, the opposite of what occurs.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Systemic (bronchial) circulation reflexively dilates to oxygenate the collapsed lung, improving arterial oxygenation
choices.rationale: Incorrect. The bronchial circulation supplies airway tissue and does not participate in gas exchange or in the autoregulatory diversion of pulmonary blood flow. The protective response is in the pulmonary arterial circulation via HPV.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Opening of oxygen-sensitive potassium channels hyperpolarizes pulmonary smooth muscle and constricts the vessels
choices.rationale: Incorrect on the channel direction. Hypoxia INHIBITS (closes) oxygen-sensitive potassium channels, causing DEPOLARIZATION (not hyperpolarization). Depolarization opens voltage-gated calcium channels, raising cytosolic calcium and producing constriction.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Systemic arterial hypoxemia, rather than alveolar hypoxia, is the primary trigger for the pulmonary vasoconstrictor response
choices.rationale: Incorrect. HPV is driven principally by low ALVEOLAR oxygen tension in the affected lung region (with a contribution from mixed venous PO2), not by systemic arterial PaO2. This local sensing is what allows region-specific blood flow diversion.

questions.explanation: Hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction (HPV) is an autoregulatory reflex in which low alveolar oxygen tension constricts the local pulmonary arterioles, diverting blood from poorly ventilated to better-ventilated lung and thereby improving ventilation-perfusion matching. During one-lung ventilation, HPV in the collapsed lung reduces its perfusion and limits the rise in shunt and the fall in PaO2. Mechanistically, hypoxia inhibits oxygen-sensitive (voltage-gated) potassium channels in pulmonary artery smooth muscle, causing membrane depolarization, voltage-gated calcium channel opening, increased cytosolic calcium, and vasoconstriction.

source.title: Physiology, Pulmonary Vasoconstriction - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf

verdict.reason:
URL loads and is publicly accessible (StatPearls NBK499962, "Physiology, Pulmonary Vasoconstriction"). Content supports the keyed answer A: the source states HPV is triggered by ALVEOLAR hypoxia (not systemic arterial hypoxemia) and "redirects blood flow within the vasculature away from poorly ventilated parts of the lungs towards better-ventilated portions" — exactly A's main clause. It also references oxygen-sensitive voltage-gated potassium and calcium channels in pulmonary artery smooth muscle. The mechanistic detail in A (inhibition/closure of O2-sensitive K+ channels → depolarization → Ca2+ influx → vasoconstriction) is the standard, unambiguous, well-established physiology; the source references these channels and contradicts none of it.

The clinical stem is a classic, correct one-lung-ventilation HPV scenario (nondependent operative lung collapsed, oxygenation better than expected for shunt — HPV limits the hypoxemia).

Distractors are accurately wrong: B ("hypoxic pulmonary vasodilation") inverts the true response (HPV is constriction); C (bronchial circulation reflexively dilating to oxygenate the collapsed lung) is physiologically false; D (K+ channels OPENING and hyperpolarizing to constrict) is internally contradictory — hyperpolarization relaxes smooth muscle — and is the inverse of the real mechanism; E is directly refuted by the source, which names alveolar (not systemic arterial) hypoxia as the trigger. Answer is correct and unambiguous.

questions.stem: A CAA is providing anesthesia at a surgical mission hospital located at 3,000 meters (barometric pressure ~525 mmHg). The anesthesia machine has a conventional agent-specific variable-bypass sevoflurane vaporizer. To maintain an adequate depth of anesthesia equivalent to what the dial setting would deliver at sea level, what is the most appropriate action regarding the vaporizer dial setting?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Leave the dial unchanged, because the delivered partial pressure of sevoflurane is essentially preserved despite the lower barometric pressure
choices.rationale: Correct. A conventional variable-bypass vaporizer delivers an output governed by saturated vapor pressure, which is a function of temperature, not ambient pressure. At altitude the volume percent (concentration) rises, but the partial pressure of agent delivered is essentially unchanged. Because anesthetic effect is determined by partial pressure in the brain, no dose adjustment is required.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increase the dial setting proportionally to the drop in barometric pressure to compensate for reduced agent delivery
choices.rationale: Incorrect. This reflects the misconception that lower ambient pressure reduces the delivered anesthetic effect. While the volume percent reading would underestimate the actual concentration, the delivered partial pressure (what determines effect) is preserved, so increasing the dial would risk overdose.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Decrease the dial setting because the displayed volume percent overstates the partial pressure delivered at altitude
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Although the volume percent does increase at altitude, the partial pressure delivered is unchanged, and partial pressure is what drives anesthetic depth. Decreasing the dial would under-dose the patient.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Switch to the desflurane (Tec 6) vaporizer, which automatically compensates partial pressure for altitude
choices.rationale: Incorrect. The Tec 6 is a heated, pressurized, dual-circuit vaporizer that holds a constant volume percent output, so at altitude the delivered partial pressure of desflurane actually DECREASES and requires an upward dial adjustment. It is the opposite of altitude-compensating for partial pressure.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Add a fixed 10% to the dial setting regardless of agent, as a universal high-altitude correction
choices.rationale: Incorrect. There is no universal fixed correction; the behavior depends on vaporizer type. Conventional variable-bypass vaporizers need no adjustment, whereas the Tec 6 desflurane vaporizer does.

questions.explanation: Conventional variable-bypass vaporizers (e.g., for sevoflurane, isoflurane) splitting-ratio output depends on the agent's saturated vapor pressure, set by temperature rather than ambient barometric pressure. At high altitude, the delivered volume percent rises but the delivered PARTIAL PRESSURE of agent stays essentially constant. Since anesthetic depth tracks partial pressure at the effect site, no dial change is needed. The exception is the Tec 6 desflurane vaporizer, which maintains a constant concentration (volume percent) and therefore delivers a LOWER partial pressure at altitude, requiring the dial to be increased.

questions.stem: An anesthesia care team is transporting an intubated, mechanically ventilated adult to a remote MRI suite and selects a Mapleson D (Bain) circuit to provide controlled positive-pressure ventilation. Which statement best explains why the Mapleson D is well suited to this scenario?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is the most efficient Mapleson configuration during controlled ventilation, requiring relatively low fresh gas flow to prevent rebreathing because fresh gas drives alveolar gas toward the APL valve
choices.rationale: Correct. In the Mapleson D, fresh gas enters near the patient and exhaled alveolar gas is pushed distally toward the APL valve, which vents it during the positive-pressure (inspiratory) phase. This makes the D the most efficient configuration for controlled ventilation, requiring comparatively modest fresh gas flow to avoid rebreathing.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It is the most efficient configuration during spontaneous ventilation, requiring fresh gas flow equal to minute ventilation
choices.rationale: Incorrect. That description applies to the Mapleson A, which is optimal for SPONTANEOUS ventilation at a fresh gas flow approximately equal to minute ventilation. The D is the efficiency mirror-image of the A.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It contains a CO2 absorber, eliminating rebreathing regardless of fresh gas flow
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Mapleson circuits have no CO2 absorber; rebreathing is prevented solely by adequate fresh gas flow. This is a defining feature distinguishing Mapleson (non-rebreathing-dependent) systems from a circle system.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Its valveless design makes it equally efficient for spontaneous and controlled ventilation, so fresh gas flow is irrelevant
choices.rationale: Incorrect. The Mapleson D has an APL valve and is decidedly NOT equally efficient in both modes; efficiency is highly mode-dependent, and fresh gas flow is the critical determinant of rebreathing in any Mapleson system.

choices.label: E
choices.text: It requires the highest fresh gas flow of all Mapleson circuits during controlled ventilation, which guarantees no rebreathing
choices.rationale: Incorrect. The premise is reversed: the D requires comparatively LOW fresh gas flow during controlled ventilation precisely because it is the most efficient configuration for that mode.

questions.explanation: Mapleson efficiency depends on ventilation mode and gas inlet geometry. The Mapleson A (fresh gas at the bag end, APL valve near the patient) is most efficient for spontaneous ventilation, needing fresh gas flow roughly equal to minute ventilation. The Mapleson D (and its coaxial Bain modification) places fresh gas near the patient, so during controlled positive-pressure ventilation alveolar gas is driven toward and vented at the distal APL valve, making it the most efficient configuration for controlled ventilation. No Mapleson circuit has a CO2 absorber, so adequate fresh gas flow is always required to prevent rebreathing.

questions.stem: During a case, a hospital facilities crew inadvertently cross-connects the oxygen and nitrous oxide pipelines, so that nitrous oxide is delivered through the machine's oxygen pipeline inlet at the normal 50 psi. The oxygen pipeline pressure gauge reads normal, the fail-safe (oxygen pressure failure) device does not activate, and the hypoxic guard permits the set flows. Which machine safety feature is most likely to first alert the team that the patient is receiving a hypoxic gas mixture?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The breathing-circuit oxygen analyzer
choices.rationale: Correct. The oxygen analyzer measures the actual fraction of oxygen in the inspired gas downstream in the breathing circuit. It is the only standard machine monitor that detects the composition of the gas (not merely its pressure), so it is the device that will catch a pipeline crossover delivering nitrous oxide in place of oxygen.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The fail-safe (oxygen pressure failure) device
choices.rationale: Incorrect. The fail-safe responds only to oxygen pipeline PRESSURE, not its identity. In a crossover the line is still pressurized at ~50 psi, so the fail-safe is satisfied and never triggers.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The proportioning (hypoxic guard) system
choices.rationale: Incorrect. The hypoxic guard mechanically links oxygen and nitrous oxide flowmeters to maintain a minimum oxygen percentage, but it assumes the gas in the oxygen line is truly oxygen. It cannot detect that nitrous oxide is present in the oxygen pipeline.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The oxygen flush valve interlock
choices.rationale: Incorrect. The oxygen flush delivers high-flow gas from the oxygen line but, like the fail-safe and hypoxic guard, has no means to verify gas identity; flushing during a crossover would deliver nitrous oxide. There is no 'interlock' that analyzes gas composition.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The pin index safety system
choices.rationale: Incorrect. The pin index safety system prevents misconnection of CYLINDERS to the wrong yoke; it has no role in pipeline crossovers and does not analyze gas composition.

questions.explanation: Most anesthesia machine oxygen safety features act on PRESSURE, not gas identity. The fail-safe device only ensures that other gases stop or decrease if oxygen pipeline pressure falls; it cannot detect that the pressurized 'oxygen' line actually contains nitrous oxide. The hypoxic guard maintains a minimum oxygen-to-nitrous-oxide flow ratio but assumes the oxygen line truly carries oxygen. The pin index system applies to cylinders, not pipelines. Only the breathing-circuit oxygen analyzer measures the actual inspired oxygen concentration, making it the definitive (and often first) monitor to detect a hypoxic delivery from a pipeline crossover, ideally before the pulse oximeter desaturates.

questions.stem: A patient in the cardiac OR has a temporary transvenous pacing wire with an exposed external connector. The anesthesia care team is concerned about microshock. Which statement most accurately characterizes the microshock hazard and the role of the operating room's line isolation monitor (LIM)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Current as low as roughly 10-100 microamperes delivered directly to the myocardium can induce ventricular fibrillation, and the LIM is not designed to protect against microshock
choices.rationale: Correct. With a direct intracardiac conductive pathway, the skin's protective resistance is bypassed and current density at the myocardium is high; current on the order of 10-100 microamperes can trigger ventricular fibrillation. The LIM monitors the integrity of the isolated power supply and alarms in the milliampere range, so it does not protect against microshock-level currents.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Microshock requires currents above 1 milliampere applied to intact skin, and the LIM reliably prevents it
choices.rationale: Incorrect. This describes macroshock thresholds (perception/let-go currents through intact skin), not microshock. Microshock involves far smaller currents delivered directly to the heart, and the LIM does not prevent microshock.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The LIM disconnects power the instant any fault current reaches the microshock threshold, eliminating the hazard
choices.rationale: Incorrect. The LIM does NOT interrupt power; it only alarms. It indicates that the isolated power system's first-fault safety margin has been lost, and its alarm threshold is in the milliampere range, far above microshock levels.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Microshock is prevented by the ground fault circuit interrupter (GFCI), which trips at microampere fault currents
choices.rationale: Incorrect. A GFCI typically trips at around 5 milliamperes to protect against macroshock; it does not act at the microampere currents relevant to microshock and is not the microshock safeguard.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Microshock can only occur if the patient simultaneously contacts two faulty devices, so a single intracardiac conductor poses no risk
choices.rationale: Incorrect. A single conductive intracardiac pathway (such as a transvenous pacing wire or saline-filled catheter) plus a small leakage current is sufficient. The exposed connector is precisely what makes the patient electrically susceptible.

questions.explanation: Microshock occurs when small currents bypass the high resistance of intact skin and are applied directly to the myocardium via a conductive pathway such as a transvenous pacing wire, pulmonary artery catheter, or saline-filled central line. Because current is concentrated at the heart, currents on the order of 10-100 microamperes can precipitate ventricular fibrillation, far below the macroshock thresholds (milliamperes) felt through intact skin. The line isolation monitor protects against macroshock by detecting loss of isolation in the ungrounded OR power supply, but it only ALARMS (it does not cut power) and operates at milliampere thresholds, so it provides no protection at microshock current levels. Protection from microshock relies on meticulous handling of intracardiac conductors, limiting equipment leakage current, and proper grounding/equipotential measures.

questions.stem: A 64-year-old man is scheduled for an arthroscopic rotator cuff repair of the right shoulder. He has severe COPD with a prior left pneumonectomy and is dyspneic on minimal exertion. The surgical team requests an interscalene brachial plexus block for postoperative analgesia. Which of the following is the most appropriate concern that should guide your anesthetic decision?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Interscalene block carries a high likelihood of ipsilateral phrenic nerve blockade and hemidiaphragmatic paresis, which may be poorly tolerated given his contralateral pneumonectomy
choices.rationale: Correct. The interscalene approach is associated with a very high incidence of ipsilateral phrenic nerve block due to cephalad spread of local anesthetic over the anterior scalene, producing hemidiaphragmatic paresis and roughly a 25% reduction in pulmonary function. In a patient with severe COPD and a contralateral pneumonectomy, losing ipsilateral diaphragm function could precipitate respiratory failure, so the block is relatively contraindicated.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Interscalene block reliably spares the diaphragm and is therefore the safest regional option in patients with respiratory compromise
choices.rationale: Incorrect. This reverses the actual physiology. Interscalene block is notorious for causing phrenic nerve blockade, not sparing it, and is explicitly cautioned against in patients with respiratory insufficiency.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The main concern is inadequate analgesia, because the interscalene block does not cover the shoulder
choices.rationale: Incorrect. The interscalene block targets the C5-C6 roots/superior trunk and provides excellent coverage of the shoulder and proximal humerus; analgesic failure for shoulder surgery is not the principal concern here. (It does tend to spare the inferior trunk, making it unreliable for hand surgery.)

choices.label: D
choices.text: The principal risk is local anesthetic systemic toxicity, which is unique to the interscalene approach
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Local anesthetic systemic toxicity is a risk of all peripheral nerve blocks and is not specific to the interscalene site; the site-specific, decision-driving hazard in this respiratory-compromised patient is phrenic nerve blockade.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Phrenic nerve involvement is permanent, so the block is contraindicated in all patients regardless of pulmonary status
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Phrenic paresis from interscalene block is typically transient and resolves as the local anesthetic wears off; it is not categorically contraindicated in everyone, only relatively contraindicated in patients who cannot tolerate the temporary loss of diaphragm function.

questions.explanation: The interscalene brachial plexus block is the gold-standard block for shoulder surgery (C5-C6/superior trunk), but it produces ipsilateral hemidiaphragmatic paresis in a very high proportion of patients because of phrenic nerve involvement, with about a 25% reduction in pulmonary function. This is generally well tolerated in healthy patients and resolves with the local anesthetic, but in a patient with severe COPD and a contralateral pneumonectomy, the loss of the only functioning hemidiaphragm could cause respiratory failure. Strategies to reduce phrenic involvement include using lower local anesthetic volumes under ultrasound guidance or choosing a more distal/diaphragm-sparing approach. The key board concept: respiratory insufficiency is a relative contraindication to interscalene block because of phrenic nerve blockade.

questions.stem: A healthy 28-year-old woman in labor has a lumbar epidural catheter placed. After negative aspiration for blood and cerebrospinal fluid, you administer a test dose of 3 mL of lidocaine 1.5% with epinephrine 1:200,000 (15 mcg epinephrine). Within about 30 seconds her heart rate rises from 80 to 105 bpm. What does this response most likely indicate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Intravascular placement of the epidural catheter
choices.rationale: Correct. An abrupt increase in heart rate of roughly 20 to 30 bpm (and/or a 15 to 20 mm Hg rise in systolic blood pressure) shortly after an epinephrine-containing test dose indicates that the epinephrine reached the systemic circulation, i.e., the catheter is intravascular. The 25 bpm rise here meets that criterion.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Subarachnoid (intrathecal) placement of the catheter
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Intrathecal placement is detected by the rapid onset of a dense motor/sensory spinal block from the lidocaine component, not by a tachycardic response to epinephrine. A high spinal would more likely cause hypotension and bradycardia, not isolated tachycardia.

choices.label: B2
choices.text: Correct epidural placement with normal systemic absorption
choices.rationale: Incorrect. A correctly positioned epidural catheter results in only slow, modest systemic uptake of epinephrine; it does not produce an abrupt 25 bpm heart-rate spike within 30 seconds. That rapid surge implies direct intravascular delivery.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A normal pain- or contraction-related maternal response that can be ignored
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Although uterine contractions can transiently raise maternal heart rate and reduce test-dose specificity, the tight temporal link to injection of an epinephrine-containing dose makes intravascular placement the most likely explanation and mandates re-evaluation before dosing.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Beta-blockade masking the true catheter position
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Beta-blockade would blunt or abolish the tachycardic response (a false-negative), making intravascular placement harder to detect. This patient mounted a robust positive response, so beta-blockade is not the explanation.

questions.explanation: The classic epinephrine-containing epidural test dose (3 mL of 1.5% lidocaine with 1:200,000 epinephrine = 15 mcg epinephrine) screens for two malpositions. The lidocaine component detects intrathecal placement (rapid dense spinal block), and the epinephrine component detects intravascular placement: a heart-rate increase of about 20 to 30 bpm or a systolic blood pressure increase of 15 to 20 mm Hg within seconds signals systemic epinephrine and therefore an intravascular catheter. Important limitations to remember: beta-adrenergic blockade can blunt the response (false negative), and in laboring patients contraction-related heart-rate variability can reduce specificity (false positive). Here, a 25 bpm jump immediately after injection is most consistent with intravascular placement, and the catheter should be repositioned or replaced.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman undergoes total knee arthroplasty. To provide postoperative analgesia while preserving the ability to participate in early physical therapy on the day of surgery, the anesthesia team performs an ultrasound-guided adductor canal block. Which feature best explains why this block is preferred over a femoral nerve block in this enhanced-recovery setting?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It primarily targets the saphenous nerve, a purely sensory branch of the femoral nerve, producing little or no quadriceps weakness
choices.rationale: Correct. The adductor canal block deposits local anesthetic around the saphenous nerve (the largest, longest purely sensory branch of the femoral nerve) and other distal sensory branches in the canal. Because these are predominantly sensory, the block provides medial knee/leg analgesia with little or no quadriceps motor weakness compared with a femoral nerve block, facilitating early mobilization.

choices.label: B
choices.text: It blocks the femoral nerve proximally, providing denser motor block than a femoral nerve block
choices.rationale: Incorrect. The adductor canal block is performed distally and is valued precisely because it spares motor function; it does not produce a denser proximal motor block. A denser quadriceps motor block would impair, not improve, early ambulation.

choices.label: C
choices.text: It provides complete anesthesia of the entire knee, including the posterior capsule, as a sole technique
choices.rationale: Incorrect. The adductor canal block covers the anteromedial knee but does not reliably anesthetize the posterior knee (often supplied by the sciatic/popliteal contribution); it is usually combined with other modalities (e.g., IPACK or multimodal analgesia) rather than serving as complete sole anesthesia.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It blocks the obturator and sciatic nerves, eliminating the need for any additional analgesia
choices.rationale: Incorrect. The adductor canal block does not reliably cover the obturator or sciatic distributions; the targeted nerve is the saphenous (femoral-derived). It is part of multimodal analgesia, not a single block that covers all knee innervation.

choices.label: E
choices.text: The saphenous nerve carries substantial motor fibers to the quadriceps, which the block strengthens
choices.rationale: Incorrect. The saphenous nerve is purely sensory and carries no motor fibers to the quadriceps; a block cannot 'strengthen' a muscle. This option misstates the fundamental anatomy that underlies the block's quadriceps-sparing benefit.

questions.explanation: The adductor canal lies in the mid-thigh, bounded by sartorius (anteriorly), vastus medialis (laterally), and the adductor muscles (posteromedially), and contains the femoral artery and vein, the saphenous nerve, and the nerve to vastus medialis. The adductor canal block targets predominantly sensory nerves, chiefly the saphenous nerve, the longest purely sensory branch of the femoral nerve. Because it spares the bulk of the motor supply to the quadriceps, it produces little or no quadriceps weakness compared with a proximal femoral nerve block, which is exactly what enhanced-recovery protocols want to enable safe, early weight-bearing and physical therapy after total knee arthroplasty. It is best used within a multimodal plan, as it does not cover the posterior knee.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman undergoes cesarean delivery under spinal anesthesia. For postoperative analgesia, bilateral ultrasound-guided transversus abdominis plane (TAP) blocks are performed. Eight hours later she reports good relief of her incisional pain but persistent deep, cramping lower-abdominal pain. Which characteristic of the TAP block best explains this pattern?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The TAP block anesthetizes the somatic afferents of the anterolateral abdominal wall but does not reliably relieve visceral pain
choices.rationale: Correct. The TAP block deposits local anesthetic in the plane between the internal oblique and transversus abdominis muscles, blocking the thoracolumbar somatic nerves (approximately T7-L1) that supply the abdominal wall. It provides reliable somatic/incisional analgesia but does not block visceral afferents, so deep cramping visceral pain (e.g., uterine) persists and requires additional analgesia.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The TAP block selectively blocks visceral afferents while sparing the somatic abdominal wall
choices.rationale: Incorrect. This is the reverse of the truth. The TAP block covers somatic abdominal wall sensation; it is the visceral component that is not reliably covered.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The residual pain proves the spinal anesthetic failed and a high spinal is developing
choices.rationale: Incorrect. The spinal was used intraoperatively and the pattern described (good wall analgesia, residual visceral cramping postoperatively) is the expected limitation of a TAP block, not evidence of an ascending or failed spinal block.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The TAP block was placed too laterally, so only the L1 nerves and not the visceral nerves were reached
choices.rationale: Incorrect. Visceral afferents do not travel in the transversus abdominis plane at all, so no adjustment of needle position within that plane would capture them. The miss is anatomic and inherent to the technique, not a placement error.

choices.label: E
choices.text: TAP blocks fail because local anesthetic cannot reach the parietal peritoneum
choices.rationale: Incorrect. The TAP block does cover the parietal peritoneum of the anterolateral abdominal wall (a somatic structure) along with skin and muscle; the limitation is specifically the visceral (organ) afferents, not the parietal peritoneum.

questions.explanation: The transversus abdominis plane block targets the anterior rami of the thoracolumbar spinal nerves (roughly T7 to L1) as they course in the neurofascial plane between the internal oblique and transversus abdominis muscles, providing analgesia to the skin, muscles, and parietal peritoneum of the anterolateral abdominal wall. Its central, board-relevant limitation is that it provides somatic (incisional) analgesia only and does not reliably cover visceral pain. After cesarean delivery, this means good relief of the wall incision but persistent deep uterine/visceral cramping, which is why TAP blocks are used as part of multimodal analgesia rather than as a stand-alone technique. The same principle applies to laparoscopy, hernia repair, and other abdominal wall surgery.

questions.stem: A 34-year-old woman undergoing elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy develops, approximately 5 minutes after administration of a rocuronium bolus, generalized urticaria, profound hypotension (systolic blood pressure 52 mmHg), tachycardia to 140/min, and a sudden rise in peak inspiratory pressures with wheezing. There is no surgical bleeding. The anesthetist suspects a Ring and Messmer grade III perioperative anaphylactic reaction. Beyond stopping the suspected trigger and calling for help, which intervention is the single most important first-line pharmacologic therapy?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Intravenous diphenhydramine 50 mg
choices.rationale: Antihistamines are second-line adjuncts; they do not reverse hypotension or bronchospasm and take 1 to 3 hours to act. Giving them first delays definitive therapy and worsens outcomes.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Epinephrine, titrated intravenously (with aggressive IV fluid resuscitation)
choices.rationale: Correct. Epinephrine is the only first-line agent in anaphylaxis. In a monitored anesthetized patient with IV access and cardiovascular collapse (grade III), titrated IV epinephrine plus aggressive volume expansion is the cornerstone; delay is associated with poorer outcomes.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Intravenous hydrocortisone 200 mg
choices.rationale: Corticosteroids are adjunctive, given largely to attenuate a possible biphasic response. They have no immediate effect on the acute cardiovascular and bronchospastic collapse and must not precede epinephrine.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Inhaled albuterol via the breathing circuit
choices.rationale: A beta-2 agonist may help residual bronchospasm but does nothing for the vasoplegia and shock that define grade III anaphylaxis. It is an adjunct after epinephrine, not the priority intervention.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Intravenous sugammadex to reverse the rocuronium
choices.rationale: Sugammadex reverses neuromuscular blockade but is not an established treatment for anaphylaxis; the reaction is an immune response, not a pharmacologic effect of paralysis, and giving it does not address shock.

questions.explanation: Perioperative anaphylaxis is commonly graded with the Ring and Messmer scale, where grades III (cardiovascular collapse) and IV (cardiac arrest) are life-threatening. Epinephrine is the only first-line drug; it reverses vasodilation, bronchospasm, and stabilizes mast cells, and delayed administration is linked to worse outcomes. In an anesthetized, monitored patient with IV access, epinephrine is titrated IV alongside aggressive fluid resuscitation because anaphylaxis causes both vasoplegia and relative hypovolemia. Antihistamines and corticosteroids are adjuncts that should never delay epinephrine. Neuromuscular blockers (rocuronium) are among the most common perioperative triggers.

questions.stem: Immediately after endotracheal intubation of a 58-year-old man with a history of poorly controlled asthma for an emergency appendectomy, the ventilator alarms for high peak inspiratory pressure. Auscultation reveals diffuse expiratory wheeze, the capnograph shows an upsloping ("shark-fin") expiratory plateau, and oxygen saturation is drifting down to 92%. Endobronchial intubation, tube kinking, and secretions have been excluded. Which is the most appropriate first-line intervention for this intraoperative bronchospasm?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Deepen the plane of anesthesia by increasing the inhaled volatile agent and administer 100% oxygen
choices.rationale: Correct. Once mechanical causes are excluded, the first and foremost step is to deepen anesthesia; volatile agents are potent bronchodilators, and increasing FiO2 to 100% addresses the desaturation.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Administer intravenous aminophylline as the initial bronchodilator
choices.rationale: Aminophylline has a narrow therapeutic window and arrhythmogenic potential and is not first-line; inhaled beta-2 agonists are preferred over aminophylline for intraoperative bronchospasm.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Extubate immediately, as the endotracheal tube is the cause
choices.rationale: Mechanical and tube-related causes have already been excluded, and the patient requires the airway for emergency surgery. Extubating a hypoxemic, anesthetized patient is dangerous and does not treat the bronchospasm.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Give intravenous dexamethasone as monotherapy and continue current ventilator settings
choices.rationale: Corticosteroids are useful adjuncts but have a delayed onset over hours; relying on steroids alone while doing nothing acutely allows ongoing hypoxemia and air-trapping.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Administer a neuromuscular blocking agent to relax the chest wall
choices.rationale: Paralysis relaxes skeletal (chest wall) muscle but does not relieve bronchial smooth-muscle constriction, which is the pathology in bronchospasm; it addresses the wrong target.

questions.explanation: Intraoperative bronchospasm presents with rising peak airway pressures, wheeze, an upsloping capnograph, and desaturation. After rapidly excluding mechanical mimics (endobronchial intubation, kinking, secretions, light anesthesia causing coughing/biting), the first and foremost step is to deepen the plane of anesthesia, because volatile agents such as sevoflurane and isoflurane are potent bronchodilators, while increasing FiO2 to 100%. Inhaled beta-2 agonists (e.g., salbutamol) plus corticosteroids are added; aminophylline is no longer favored as first-line. Asthma confers airway hyperresponsiveness that raises the risk of bronchospasm on airway instrumentation.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman with chronic kidney disease receives 2 units of packed red blood cells over 90 minutes for a hemoglobin of 6.5 g/dL during a hip arthroplasty. About 3 hours into the transfusion she develops acute dyspnea, bilateral pulmonary infiltrates on chest radiograph, and hypoxemia. She is afebrile, normotensive-to-hypertensive, has jugular venous distension, and her NT-proBNP is markedly elevated compared with a pre-transfusion value. Which feature most strongly favors transfusion-associated circulatory overload (TACO) over transfusion-related acute lung injury (TRALI)?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The markedly elevated post-transfusion NT-proBNP with signs of volume overload
choices.rationale: Correct. Elevated BNP/NT-proBNP plus signs of circulatory overload (JVD, hypertension) point to a hydrostatic/cardiogenic mechanism characteristic of TACO; in TRALI these natriuretic peptides are typically normal.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The presence of bilateral pulmonary infiltrates with hypoxemia
choices.rationale: Both TACO and TRALI produce bilateral infiltrates and hypoxemia, so this finding does not discriminate between them.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Onset of symptoms within 6 hours of transfusion
choices.rationale: Both reactions occur within hours of transfusion (TRALI classically within 6 hours, TACO commonly within 6 to 12 hours), so timing alone in this window does not distinguish them.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Transient leukopenia on the complete blood count
choices.rationale: Transient leukopenia, along with fever and hypotension, is a feature that favors TRALI, not TACO; this would point away from the correct diagnosis.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Improvement in oxygenation after the patient receives a fluid bolus
choices.rationale: TACO is volume overload; it improves with diuresis, not with additional fluid. A favorable response to diuretics (not to fluid loading) supports TACO.

questions.explanation: TACO and TRALI both cause acute hypoxemic respiratory distress with bilateral infiltrates within hours of transfusion, making them difficult to distinguish at the bedside. TACO is hydrostatic pulmonary edema from volume overload: patients show signs of circulatory overload (JVD, hypertension), an elevated BNP/NT-proBNP, and improve with diuretics. TRALI is increased-permeability lung injury from donor antibodies activating recipient neutrophils; natriuretic peptides are typically normal, and patients more often have fever, hypotension, and transient leukopenia, and require fluid resuscitation rather than diuresis. The elderly patient with renal disease receiving a rapid 2-unit load is a classic TACO setup.

source.title: Chronicle of Hypoxemia: Transfusion-Associated Circulatory Overload Versus Transfusion-Related Acute Lung Injury - PMC

verdict.reason:
VERIFIED — publish. The cited URL (https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9440191/) loads and is publicly accessible, and its content supports the keyed answer and every distractor rationale.

Answer A (markedly elevated NT-proBNP with signs of volume overload) is correct: the article states elevated BNP/NT-proBNP "is helpful when combined with other clinical information" and is a defining TACO feature, with the case showing a markedly elevated NT-proBNP plus a dilated IVC indicating volume overload. This is the single best discriminator from TRALI.

Distractor accuracy is confirmed by the source:
- B (bilateral infiltrates + hypoxemia): non-discriminating — "Both TACO and TRALI patients have similar presentations with acute hypoxia, dyspnea... and diffuse bilateral infiltrates on chest x-ray."
- C (onset within 6 hours): non-discriminating — both conditions are defined by onset within 6 hours.
- D (transient leukopenia): actually favors TRALI, not TACO — "Unlike TACO patients, patients with TRALI may have a fever, hypotension, and transient leukopenia."
- E (improvement after fluid bolus): points away from TACO — TACO is managed with diuresis/fluid removal ("responded well to the diuresis"), whereas TRALI requires fluid resuscitation.

The medicine is correct and unambiguous, and the vignette (afebrile, normotensive-to-hypertensive, JVD, elevated NT-proBNP, CKD, recent 2-unit transfusion) is internally consistent with the TACO profile. No issues found.

questions.stem: During a posterior fossa craniotomy performed in the sitting position, a 45-year-old patient suddenly develops a sharp drop in end-tidal CO2, a "mill-wheel" murmur on precordial Doppler, hypotension, and desaturation, consistent with a significant venous air embolism. The surgeon is asked to flood the field with saline and the patient is given 100% oxygen. In addition to these measures and supporting the circulation, which patient-positioning maneuver is most appropriate?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Place the patient in the left lateral decubitus and Trendelenburg (head-down) position
choices.rationale: Correct. The left lateral decubitus (Durant) plus Trendelenburg position is used to overcome right-ventricular air lock by trapping air at the RV apex away from the outflow tract, allowing the heart to continue ejecting blood.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Place the patient in the right lateral decubitus, head-up position
choices.rationale: Right lateral, head-up positioning encourages air toward the right-ventricular outflow tract and pulmonary artery, worsening the air lock; it is the opposite of the recommended maneuver.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sit the patient fully upright to encourage air to rise out of the cranial veins
choices.rationale: Maintaining or increasing the upright position keeps the surgical site above the heart, promoting continued air entrainment; the head-up sitting position is the setup that predisposes to VAE.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Place the patient prone to compress the venous sinuses
choices.rationale: Turning an anesthetized neurosurgical patient with an open cranium prone is impractical and dangerous during an acute crisis and is not an established maneuver for relieving right-heart air lock.

choices.label: E
choices.text: Apply steep reverse Trendelenburg to lower intracranial venous pressure
choices.rationale: Reverse Trendelenburg (head-up) lowers venous pressure at the wound and favors more air entrainment; it does not relieve the right-ventricular air lock and is counterproductive.

questions.explanation: Venous air embolism is a feared complication of procedures where the surgical site is above the heart (e.g., sitting craniotomy). Signs include an abrupt fall in end-tidal CO2, a mill-wheel murmur, hypotension, and desaturation. Management includes flooding the field, lowering the head to raise venous pressure at the wound, 100% oxygen, hemodynamic support, and aspirating air through a pre-existing central catheter. The left lateral decubitus (Durant) plus Trendelenburg position is employed to overcome right-ventricular air lock that impedes ejection, by directing the air bubble away from the right-ventricular outflow tract. Note that emergent central line placement solely to aspirate air is not supported if a line is not already present.

source.title: Venous Gas Embolism - StatPearls - NCBI Bookshelf

verdict.reason: The cited URL (https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK482249/, "Venous Gas Embolism" StatPearls) loads, is publicly accessible, and directly supports the keyed answer A. It explicitly states: "The left lateral decubitus (Durant) and Trendelenburg positions are employed to overcome right ventricular air lock," matching option A (left lateral decubitus + Trendelenburg/head-down). The source also corroborates the stem's adjunct measures: "Flooding the operative field with normal saline can limit further air entry" and "Administration of 100% oxygen should be initiated as early as possible," plus the diagnostic mill-wheel murmur and precordial Doppler. The medicine is correct and unambiguous: the Durant maneuver (left lateral decubitus, head-down) is standard, well-established management for venous air embolism to trap air at the RV apex and relieve the air lock. All distractors are accurately wrong: B (right lateral decubitus, head-up) has both wrong side and wrong head position; C (sit fully upright) is head-up, worsens the situation, and does not relieve the air lock; D (prone) is not a recognized maneuver; E (reverse Trendelenburg/head-up) is the opposite of the indicated head-down position. The question is sound and publishable.
